games.explanation: The market is treating this like a coin flip because both teams are bad, but only one of them is sending out a starter who can't get lefties out. Brandon Young has been a different pitcher against left-handed bats, and Washington happens to roll out James Wood, García, and Abrams in a row to greet him. Wood in particular is the nightmare matchup, a lefty barreling everything in sight against an arm whose hard contact problem gets worse from that side. Getting +110 on the better team in this game, with the pitching edge tilted their way, is the entire ballgame.

Skenes is the best pitcher on the planet right now and the market is pricing this like that fact alone wins baseball games, but Philadelphia is the wrong opponent to test that theory against. Wheeler matches him pitch for pitch on the other side, and a Phillies lineup leaning heavily left has the exact profile that's chipped Skenes' armor all year (his split against lefties is nearly 60 points of xwOBA worse than against righties). Schwarber and Harper don't care that the other guy throws 100, they care about barrels, and they've combined for the kind of damage rate that doesn't disappear because of velocity. Getting plus money on the better roster in a projected one-run game is the entire argument, and at +119 it makes itself.

Kansas City is sending up a swingman who has spent the year getting tattooed by left-handed bats, and St. Louis happens to roll out a lineup full of them. Stephen Kolek's splits are not subtle, with lefties posting a .343 xwOBA against him while barreling him at a near-9% clip, and Iván Herrera in particular has been a different human against this exact pitch profile. Add 15 mph of wind blowing straight in to neutralize whatever damage Pallante might give back, and the run environment tilts toward the side with the better roster. At -114, the market is pricing this like a coin flip between evenly matched clubs, which is not what tonight is.

The market is treating this like a coin flip with a Dodgers tax, which ignores that one of these lineups is built to punish wildness and the other is built to provide it. Sasaki's walk rate has been the giveaway all year, and the Angels do not have the patient, slugging core to make him pay for nibbling, while Ohtani gets to face exactly the kind of right-handed arm he has been torching to a .452 xwOBA. The wind blowing in flatters the worse pitcher here, not the better lineup, and that lineup is still a multi-run favorite on the projection. -136 to back the team that's better at every layer of this game is the easy part.

Kirby's reputation hasn't caught up to the fact that he's been a different pitcher this year, and the market is still pricing him like the ace he used to be. Right-handed bats have tagged him for nearly a 47% hard-hit rate, which is bad news when the visiting lineup runs Tatis, Machado, and Merrill at you in order. Giolito isn't anyone's idea of a stopper, but a closed roof in T-Mobile suppresses the one thing he actually fears, and the Mariners lineup outside of Julio doesn't punish mistakes the way San Diego's does. Getting +139 on the better team, with the worse starter wearing the home jersey, is the kind of mismatch the board only hands you when the names on the marquee are doing the work.

Getting plus money on the better roster in a projected one-run game is the entire argument, and at +119 it makes itself.

Cleveland is favored here because the names look prettier, not because the gap between these teams is anywhere near what the price suggests. Gavin Williams gets paid for the strikeouts but walks the ballpark, and a Reds lineup that runs Benson, De La Cruz, and a scorching Sal Stewart at the top is built to make a command-dependent righty pay for every nibble. The wind is blowing in and the park plays neutral tonight, which is exactly the low-scoring framework where a run and a half travels a long way. Sims land on a half-run Cleveland edge, the market is pricing closer to two, and -145 on the plus side of that gap is the entire argument.
